_meta.author: scorecard-refine-local autopilot
_meta.note: Evidence-finalize Dallas TX local batch (jaime-resendez, jesse-moreno, john-creuzot, kathy-stewart, laura-cadena). LOCAL tier, social-media + local-press + council-vote protocol. Currency verified for all five (May 2025 council cycle; Creuzot lost Mar 2026 DA primary, sits through Dec 2026).

profile.education: Eastfield College; B.S. criminal justice, UNT-Dallas; J.D., UT School of Law
profile.background: Pleasant Grove native; U.S. Army combat veteran (8 years, year in Baghdad); criminal-defense/personal-injury attorney; Dallas ISD trustee (D4) 2016-2019. First elected to council 2019; re-elected May 2025 (84% over Elizabeth Matus) — fourth and final term under Dallas's 4-term limit.

jaime-resendez.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 with 84%; term-limited after this term. Present for the 13-1 GRACE Act vote (Aug 2022, deprioritizing abortion investigations; sole no was McGough — Resendez among the yes votes). Stonewall Democrats of Dallas endorsement. Defund-era record: voted to cut $7M DPD overtime (Sept 2020) and was one of two votes against restoring a $10M police-overtime request (2021); publicly opposed 2024 Dallas HERO Prop U (4,000-officer mandate). Co-authored Oct 2025 statement against DPD joining ICE 287(g) ('a betrayal of trust'); council rejected the partnership. No findable positions on schools, religious liberty, elections, state-overreach cells, or guns — left null. Religion not stated in any source found.

0.note: Present for the 13-1 GRACE Act vote (Aug 10, 2022) deprioritizing abortion-related investigations; the sole no vote was McGough and the three absent members are named — Resendez was among the yes votes.

1.note: Voted to deprioritize abortion enforcement — documented contrary to abolition.

0.note: Endorsed by Stonewall Democrats of Dallas (LGBTQ Democratic org) — single-issue endorsement contrary to a man-woman-only marriage affirmation.

2.note: No trans-specific statements found — left null.

4.note: Stonewall Democrats endorsement — accepted single-issue LGBTQ-advocacy endorsement, contrary to opposing LGBTQ+ promotion in public policy.

0.note: Voted yes on the Sept 2020 amendment cutting $7M from DPD overtime; one of only two votes against restoring a $10M police-overtime mechanism (2021); publicly opposed 2024 Prop U police-staffing mandate. No affirmative pro-police advocacy found.

1.note: Co-authored statement that DPD joining ICE 287(g) 'could result in a betrayal of trust'; called the mayor's push 'a callous political stunt' — documented contrary to supporting mandatory deportation.

2.note: Led opposition to and voted to reject the $25M DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership (Oct-Nov 2025) — documented contrary to supporting local-federal immigration enforcement cooperation.

profile.background: Co-owner of family restaurants La Popular Tamale House and Peak & Elm; third-generation District 2 resident; 7+ years on the Dallas Park Board. First elected 2021; re-elected May 2025 over Sukhbir Kaur; appointed Mayor Pro Tem June 16, 2025 (one-year term).

jesse-moreno.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025; Mayor Pro Tem since June 16, 2025 (annual reselection due ~June 2026). Absent for the 2022 GRACE Act vote but publicly endorsed it. Read the city's 2025 Pride Month proclamation. Deviation from progressive expectation: one of only 3 no votes on the FY2025-26 $5.5B budget, dissenting for deeper spending cuts and a bigger tax-rate reduction; and genuine affirmative pro-police advocacy in 2025 — unveiled the Deep Ellum crime plan (more police, mounted patrol) and pushed to RAISE DPD hiring goals — though he voted to pull $10M DPD overtime in 2021 and opposed 2024 Prop U (mixed record, credited on the recent affirmative advocacy). Opposed DPD joining ICE 287(g) (Oct 2025). Created/championed the 2023 Dallas gun-buyback program (voluntary; matches no specific self-defense cell — noted, left null). Religion/education not found.

0.note: Absent for the GRACE Act vote but publicly endorsed it: police shouldn't spend resources 'hunting down people who are simply trying to get health care, and abortion is part of that category.'

1.note: Advocates protecting abortion access — documented contrary to abolition.

4.note: As Mayor Pro Tem read the city's 2025 Pride Month proclamation ('inclusion must be a continuous and intentional effort') and spoke at the Love Field Pride flag unveiling — official promotion of LGBTQ+ identity in public policy.

2.note: One of only 3 no votes (with Mayor Johnson and Mendelsohn) on the FY2025-26 $5.5B budget — dissent attributed to wanting deeper spending cuts and a larger tax-rate reduction.

0.note: Affirmative advocacy: personally unveiled the 2025 Deep Ellum crime plan (more police, mounted patrol, cameras) and pushed to raise DPD hiring goals ('The only way we achieve a higher number is by setting a higher goal'). Mixed: voted to pull $10M DPD overtime (2021) and opposed 2024 Prop U — credited on the sustained recent affirmative record.

1.note: Opposed DPD-ICE 287(g) despite $25M federal offer: 'we don't want to go backwards' on community trust; said immigration enforcement is the federal government's job — contrary to supporting mandatory deportation.

2.note: Publicly opposed the local-federal immigration enforcement partnership; committees killed it Nov 6, 2025.

1.note: Created/championed the 2023 Dallas gun-buyback program — gun-reduction advocacy, but voluntary and not a red-flag law, ban, limit, or registry; does not match this cell's specific policies. Left null, flagged here.

profile.background: New Orleans-born; former Dallas ADA and chief felony prosecutor; appointed district judge 1991 (by Gov. Ann Richards); ran as a Republican 1996-2008 before returning to the Democratic Party; pioneered drug-court diversion as a judge. DA since 2019. Lost the March 3, 2026 Democratic primary to former judge Amber Givens 54-46; no Republican filed, so Givens is the de facto next DA. Sitting DA through term end Dec 31, 2026.

john-creuzot.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier, DA). Currency verified: lost Mar 3, 2026 primary to Amber Givens (54-46, no runoff); remains sitting DA through Dec 31, 2026 — candidacy_status set to lost, status remains active while in office. Signed the June 2022 Fair and Just Prosecution post-Dobbs pledge to 'refrain from prosecuting those who seek, provide, or support abortions' (later softened to case-by-case). Refused the 2022 Abbott/Paxton directive treating gender-affirming care for minors as child abuse. 2019 reform memo: declined first-time marijuana, theft of necessities under $750, homeless criminal trespass; called county bail unconstitutional; Texas Republicans cited him by name when passing HB 17 (2023). Walk-backs noted: rescinded the theft policy Nov 2022 ('zero effect on crime... more aspirational than realistic'); post-HB 17 said 'The law has changed. We have to prosecute every case.' His office won the Amber Guyger murder conviction. Sued AG Paxton (May 2025) over oversight rules; joined the FAFO anti-federal-overreach prosecutor coalition (Jan 2026) warning ICE agents they lack absolute immunity. Existing Soros-PAC score adjustment ($750K+ Texas Justice & Public Safety PAC) retained. Refuse-state-overreach cells left null: his resistance is to state criminal-law oversight, which the rubric does not credit.

0.note: Signed the June 2022 FJP joint statement committing to refrain from prosecuting abortion: 'my office will not stand in the way of [women] seeking the health care they need.'

1.note: Pledged non-prosecution of Texas abortion law (later softened to case-by-case discretion) — documented contrary to abolition.

2.note: Joined 4 other Texas DAs refusing the Abbott/Paxton directive on gender-affirming care for minors: 'my office will not be prosecuting parents or medical providers in accordance with this harmful directive' — documented contrary to rejecting transgender ideology.

1.note: 2019 reform memo declined whole classes of crime (first-time marijuana, theft under $750, homeless trespass) and attacked cash bail as unconstitutional; Texas Republicans cited Creuzot by name when passing HB 17 (2023) to rein in non-prosecuting DAs. Theft policy rescinded Nov 2022 — noted, but the policy record is documented.

2.note: Categorical declination of theft under $750 ('necessary items') is documented contrary to proportional punishment; he himself rescinded it in Nov 2022 saying it had 'zero effect on crime' and was 'more aspirational than realistic.'

1.note: Joined the FAFO prosecutor coalition (Jan 2026) formed against federal/ICE enforcement actions, warning agents 'you do not have absolute immunity' — contrary to supporting mandatory deportation.

profile.background: Estate-planning/probate attorney; owned Highlands Café 2004-2014; executive director of the Lake Highlands Public Improvement Districts (2014-2019) and Uptown Dallas Inc. (2019-2022); longtime Richardson ISD PTA president and Lake Highlands HS Foundation leader. First elected 2023; re-elected May 2025 with ~92.5%. Vice Chair, Public Safety Committee.

kathy-stewart.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 (~92.5%), sworn in June 2025. PARTY FLAG: the 'R' label is unsubstantiated — Dallas council is nonpartisan, she told a candidate questionnaire 'it's essential to remain nonpartisan,' and her endorsements were business/real-estate (TREC PAC, MetroTex Realtors), not partisan; label retained but unverified. Genuine affirmative pro-police record: proposed the 325-officer/yr DPD hiring-goal compromise (Feb 2025), praised the police/fire pension funding deal for ending recruitment 'uncertainty,' championed the neighborhood police officer program (Dec 2025). Opposed Dallas HERO Props S/T/U on budget grounds shared with the police union ('devastating to the city budget... and to police officers who could lose their immunity'). 287(g): issued a neutral statement supporting officers and the chief; joined the unanimous committee postponement that shelved the ICE partnership — left null (declining a voluntary partnership is not a sanctuary policy; individual roll call unverified). Co-signed concerns the 2024 marijuana-decriminalization charter amendment 'appears to be straight-up illegal' (legality concern, not scored). Voted for the FY2025-26 budget with the 10th straight tax-rate cut (routine governance, not scored). No findable positions on abortion, marriage/gender, school choice, religious liberty, elections, state overreach, or guns — left null; sparse denominator noted. Religion not documented.

0.note: Affirmative advocacy as Public Safety Vice Chair: proposed the 325-officer/yr DPD hiring goal (Feb 2025), praised the police/fire pension fix for ending recruitment 'uncertainty,' and championed the neighborhood police officer program (Dec 2025). Prop U opposition was on budget grounds shared with the police union — not defund-side.

profile.education: B.A., Baylor University; M.Div., Truett Theological Seminary (Baylor)
profile.background: Fourth-generation West Dallas; ~8 years as chief of staff for the District 6 council office (not an attorney); Christian K-12 schooling. Won D6 outright May 3, 2025 with 50.43% over former councilmember Monica Alonzo (recount confirmed); assumed office June 16, 2025. Endorsed by Our Revolution, Latino Victory Fund, SEIU.

laura-cadena.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: won May 2025 outright (50.43%, recount upheld), seated June 16, 2025 — first-year member with a thin record; scored from her own platform, official acts, and council votes. Background corrected: former D6 chief of staff, not attorney; M.Div. from Baylor's Truett Seminary. Campaign platform: 'Hire more police officers & strengthen community trust' plus retention incentives — affirmative pro-police advocacy, reinforced in office (Dec 2025 praise for the neighborhood police officer program). Joint statement with Mayor Pro Tem Moreno opposing the DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership (Oct 2025): immigration enforcement 'is the responsibility of the federal government.' Gave official remarks at the city's 2026 Pride in Excellence Awards. Voted yes on the FY2025-26 budget (routine governance, not scored). Our Revolution endorsement is a soft progressive signal — not scored. No findable positions on abortion, school choice, religious liberty, elections, state overreach, or guns — left null. Religion not explicitly stated (M.Div. noted in background).

4.note: Gave official 'special remarks' at the City of Dallas 2026 Pride in Excellence Awards honoring LGBTQ+ leaders (June 4, 2026) — official participation in city promotion of LGBTQ+ identity.

0.note: Platform plank in her own words: 'Hire more police officers & strengthen community trust,' plus police retention incentives; in office she praised continued investment in the neighborhood police officer program (Dec 2025). Meets the affirmative-advocacy bar.

1.note: Joint statement opposing DPD-ICE 287(g): immigration enforcement 'is the responsibility of the federal government' and the program 'could result in a betrayal of trust' — contrary to supporting mandatory deportation.

2.note: Publicly opposed the local-federal immigration enforcement partnership; committees shelved it Nov 6, 2025.
